content.text: Setting SLO Targets: Starting From Observed Behavior

An SLO target that is too tight triggers constant alerts and trains engineers to ignore them. One that is too loose provides no signal until the service is severely degraded. For a newly launched service with no historical baseline, the right starting point is: observe production performance for the first two weeks, set targets below the best observed performance, and tighten over subsequent quarters as confidence grows.

For GenAI services, this process is complicated by quality SLOs — there is no pre-launch baseline for eval pass rate because the service has not yet seen real user queries. The initial quality SLO target is set from pre-launch eval on a representative sample, then adjusted as production query patterns are understood.

attrs.alt: Error budget visualization and burn rate alert thresholds for four SLO categories

content.text: How to Set Each SLO Target
The standard availability SLO tiers map to engineering criticality:

For most GenAI features that are user-facing, 99.9% is the right starting point. Setting 99.99% requires significant investment in redundancy and fallback paths that is only justified when the feature is on the critical path for revenue or safety. Start at 99.9% and tighten after demonstrating the architecture can sustain it.

The availability SLO target must be set based on observed baseline performance, not aspiration. If the service achieves 99.95% in load testing, the initial SLO should be 99.9% — 0.05% below the observed baseline. Setting it at 99.95% leaves no room for the traffic patterns and failure modes that will be discovered in production.
The latency SLO is set on the P99 of the latency distribution, not the mean. The target is typically 1.5x the observed P99 baseline from pre-launch load testing:
If load testing shows P99 TTLT = 1.7s, set the SLO at 2.5s.
If load testing shows P95 TTFT = 500ms, set the TTFT SLO at 750ms.

The 1.5x multiplier accounts for the variance between load test conditions (controlled traffic, no real user behavior) and production (bursty traffic, longer tail queries, cold starts). After 30 days of production data, recalibrate using actual P99 measurements.
The eval pass rate SLO has no pre-launch analog for other service types — it is unique to GenAI. The process:
Run the eval suite against a representative sample of expected user queries before launch. This gives a baseline pass rate.
Subtract 5 percentage points for the SLO target. If pre-launch eval gives 97%, the initial SLO is 92%.
After 30 days of production data, adjust. The production query distribution almost always differs from the pre-launch sample. Tighten the SLO to within 3pp of the observed 30-day average pass rate.
Review quarterly. As the prompt is refined and the eval suite matures, the target should rise. A mature GenAI service should have an eval SLO within 1-2pp of its average performance.

Do not set the initial eval SLO equal to the pre-launch baseline. Production query distributions are harder than curated test sets. The first 30 days of production will almost certainly reveal failure modes that lower the observed pass rate below the pre-launch number.

Safety SLOs are binary: either the rate is below the threshold, or it is a critical incident. There is no error budget for safety — a breach always requires immediate investigation. The threshold itself is set at:
Start at 0.05% (5 in 10,000 requests) as a baseline for general-purpose GenAI features.
Lower to 0.01% for features that handle sensitive domains (healthcare, legal, children).
The threshold is not adjusted upward based on observed rates. If the observed rate is above the threshold, fix the underlying issue rather than raising the threshold.
Error Budget: The Quantification of Risk Tolerance
The error budget is the complement of the SLO target: the fraction of requests (or time, for availability) that is allowed to miss the target before the SLO is breached.
6% of sampled responses may fail eval

Error budgets quantify how much risk the organization is willing to accept in exchange for development velocity. When the error budget is full, the team can deploy changes aggressively. When it is nearly exhausted, deployments should pause until the budget recovers. This creates a data-driven mechanism for the tension between reliability and feature velocity.
Burn Rate: How Fast Is the Budget Being Consumed?

The burn rate measures how quickly the error budget is being consumed relative to the rate it would need to be consumed to exactly exhaust the budget at the end of the SLO window. A burn rate of 1.0 means the budget is being consumed at exactly the rate that would exhaust it at the window boundary — no headroom. A burn rate of 2.0 means it would be exhausted halfway through the window.

The 14.4x threshold is derived from the SRE literature: at 14.4x burn rate, 30 days of error budget is consumed in 2 days (30 / 14.4 ≈ 2.08). This is the threshold that requires an immediate page because the budget will be gone before a weekly review cycle can respond.

A single burn rate alert window creates two types of noise. A very short window (5 minutes) alerts on transient spikes that resolve on their own. A very long window (6 hours) is too slow to catch acute outages before significant budget is consumed. The solution is to require both a fast and a slow window to exceed the threshold before firing:

# monitoring/burn_rate_alerts.yaml
# Multi-window burn rate alerts for availability SLO (99.9%)
# Pattern: two windows that must both exceed the burn rate threshold.
# Fast window catches acute outages; slow window catches slow burns.
groups:
  - name: genai.slo.burn_rate
    rules:
      # Page-now alert: 14.4x burn rate exhausts budget in 2 days
      - alert: AvailabilitySLOBurnRateCritical
        expr: |
          (
            1 - sli:availability:ratio_1h > (1 - 0.999) * 14.4
          ) and (
            1 - sli:availability:ratio_5m > (1 - 0.999) * 14.4
          )
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          slo: availability
        annotations:
          summary: 'Availability burn rate critical (14.4x)'
          description: |
            Current burn rate will exhaust the 30-day error budget
            in approximately 2 days. Immediate investigation required.
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki/runbooks/availability-slo-breach.md'

      # Warning: 6x burn rate exhausts budget in 5 days
      - alert: AvailabilitySLOBurnRateHigh
        expr: |
          (
            1 - sli:availability:ratio_6h > (1 - 0.999) * 6
          ) and (
            1 - sli:availability:ratio_30m > (1 - 0.999) * 6
          )
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          slo: availability
        annotations:
          summary: 'Availability burn rate elevated (6x)'
          description: 'Budget exhaustion in ~5 days at current rate.'

      # Eval pass rate SLO burn rate (different calculation — SLO is 94%)
      - alert: EvalPassRateSLOBurnRateHigh
        expr: |
          (
            (0.94 - sli:eval_pass_rate:avg_7d) / (1 - 0.94) > 6
          )
        for: 30m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          slo: eval_pass_rate
        annotations:
          summary: 'Eval pass rate SLO burn rate elevated'
          runbook_url: 'https://wiki/runbooks/eval-pass-rate-degradation.md'
Setting Initial Targets: A Decision Framework
The following function encodes the target-setting heuristics as executable code, making the decision process transparent and repeatable:

# scripts/set_slo_targets.py
# Decision framework for setting initial SLO targets based on
# observed performance and traffic profile.
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class SLORecommendation:
    slo_name: str
    observed_p50: float
    observed_p99: float
    recommendation: float
    rationale: str

def recommend_latency_slo(p99_baseline_s: float) -> SLORecommendation:
    # Standard practice: SLO target = baseline P99 * 1.5
    # Gives headroom for traffic spikes and model variability
    # while still catching genuine regressions.
    target = round(p99_baseline_s * 1.5, 1)
    return SLORecommendation(
        slo_name='p99_latency',
        observed_p50=p99_baseline_s * 0.4,
        observed_p99=p99_baseline_s,
        recommendation=target,
        rationale=(
            f'Baseline P99 = {p99_baseline_s}s. '
            f'SLO at {target}s (1.5x baseline) catches '
            'regressions while tolerating natural variance.'
        ),
    )

def recommend_availability_slo(baseline_rate: float,
                               criticality: str) -> SLORecommendation:
    # Map criticality to minimum SLO floor
    floors = {'low': 0.99, 'medium': 0.999, 'high': 0.9999}
    target = max(baseline_rate * 0.999, floors[criticality])
    target = round(target, 4)
    return SLORecommendation(
        slo_name='availability',
        observed_p50=baseline_rate,
        observed_p99=baseline_rate,
        recommendation=target,
        rationale=(
            f'Baseline availability = {baseline_rate:.4f}. '
            f'Criticality={criticality}, floor={floors[criticality]}. '
            f'SLO set to {target}.'
        ),
    )

# For eval pass rate: start with observed baseline minus 5pp buffer
def recommend_eval_slo(baseline_pass_rate: float) -> SLORecommendation:
    target = round(baseline_pass_rate - 0.05, 2)
    return SLORecommendation(
        slo_name='eval_pass_rate',
        observed_p50=baseline_pass_rate,
        observed_p99=baseline_pass_rate,
        recommendation=target,
        rationale=(
            f'Baseline eval = {baseline_pass_rate:.2f}. '
            f'SLO at {target:.2f} (5pp below baseline) '
            'tolerates natural day-to-day variance.'
        ),
    )

# Example
print(recommend_latency_slo(1.7))
print(recommend_availability_slo(0.9995, 'high'))
print(recommend_eval_slo(0.97))
SLOs should be reviewed and tightened quarterly. The tightening process follows a consistent pattern:
Actual SLI values vs initial targets
Budget consumption rate, false alert rate
Year of production data, peer benchmarks
Align with industry standard for service tier

Do not tighten in response to a single good month. Tighten only when at least two consecutive measurement windows show the service sustainably operating well above the current SLO. A premature tightening consumes the error budget buffer and creates alert fatigue during the next incident.

Never loosen an SLO in response to a breach. An SLO breach means the service failed to meet its reliability commitment, not that the commitment was too strict. Loosening after a breach removes the accountability signal and hides the root cause from the error budget tracking system.
The SLA is derived from the SLO by applying a buffer that accounts for measurement error, reporting lag, and the time needed to remediate before a formal breach is declared. Standard practice:
Eval pass rate SLA = SLO - 3pp (e.g., 94% SLO → 91% SLA)

The buffer ensures that SRE can detect an SLO breach, investigate, and remediate before the customer-facing SLA is breached. Without this buffer, every SLO alert is also an SLA breach — removing the ability to fix issues before they become contractual violations.

SLO targets for GenAI services are set from observed baseline performance — not aspirational numbers — using service criticality tiers for availability (99.9% for most user-facing features), 1.5x P99 for latency, and baseline minus 5pp for eval pass rate. The error budget is the complement of the SLO target and quantifies the organization's risk tolerance. Burn rate alerts use multi-window thresholds (14.4x for immediate page, 6x for watch, 3x for monitor) to distinguish acute outages from slow degradations. SLOs are reviewed quarterly and tightened when the service sustains performance well above the target; they are never loosened in response to a breach. The SLA is always set looser than the SLO by a buffer that gives SRE time to remediate before a customer-facing commitment is violated.
